column1: This domain covers problem framing, algorithm selection, training, tuning, and evaluation - the core of the ML exam.
- Simple rules can solve it (if-else logic)
- Cost of errors is too high without human review
- A lookup table or heuristic works
- **Use cases:** Robotics, game AI, resource optimization
- **Input:** CSV (no header, target first column) or LibSVM or Parquet
- **Instance:** ml.m5 (CPU) for most; ml.p3 (GPU) for large datasets
- **Framework mode:** Use as a framework (script mode) for custom logic
- **Built-in mode:** Use as built-in algorithm (simpler, no code)

column3: High training error, high validation error
column4: More features, more complex model, less regularization

column3: Low training error, high validation error
column4: More data, regularization, dropout, simpler model

column3: Low training error, low validation error (similar)

column1: - **K-Fold Cross Validation:** Rotate validation set K times (small datasets)
- **Time Series:** NEVER random split - use temporal ordering
- **Training error decreasing, validation error increasing** = Overfitting
- **Both errors low and close** = Good fit

column3: Concurrent jobs (more = faster but less Bayesian benefit)
Reuse results from previous tuning jobs

column4: Models objective as Gaussian Process, explores intelligently
Random sampling, no learning between jobs
Early stopping of bad configs, allocates more to promising

column1: - Start with Random search to identify promising regions
- Limit parallel jobs for Bayesian (it learns from previous results)
- Use log-scaled ranges for learning rate
- Don't tune too many parameters at once (< 10)

column4: Cost of false positives is high (spam filter)
Cost of false negatives is high (cancer detection)

column1: - **Type I Error (FP):** False alarm (predicted positive, actually negative)
- **Type II Error (FN):** Missed detection (predicted negative, actually positive)

column4: Must catch all fraud (FN is costly)
Don't want legitimate email in spam (FP is costly)
Must not miss disease (FN is dangerous)
Too high = diverge, too low = slow

column3: Gradients approach zero in deep nets
Adaptive learning rate, fast convergence (default choice)
Train on random subsets, average predictions
Sequential, each model fixes previous errors
Train meta-model on base model outputs
Split data across GPUs, sync gradients
column4: Large datasets, model fits in one GPU
Model too large for one GPU

column1: 3. Replace final layer(s) for your task
- **NLP:** Hugging Face pre-trained models (BERT, GPT, T5)
- **""Image classification""** -> Image Classification (ResNet) or transfer learning
- **""Fix overfitting""** -> More data, regularization (L1/L2), dropout, early stopping, simpler model
- **""Fix underfitting""** -> More features, more complex model, less regularization, more training time
- **""XGBoost overfitting""** -> Reduce max_depth, increase min_child_weight, reduce eta, add regularization
- **""Spam filter metric""** -> Precision (don't block good email)
- **""Imbalanced data metric""** -> F1 or AUC-PR (NOT accuracy)
- **""Regression metric""** -> RMSE (penalize large errors) or MAE (robust)
- **""Vanishing gradient""** -> ReLU activation, LSTM, residual connections
- **XGBoost is the answer** for most tabular data questions. Know its parameters cold.
- **Bayesian HPO** is the default and best strategy for SageMaker tuning.
- **Recall for fraud/medical.** Precision for spam. F1 for balance. AUC-PR for imbalanced.
- **Overfitting = high variance.** Fix with regularization, dropout, more data, early stopping.
- **Underfitting = high bias.** Fix with more features, more complex model, less regularization.
- **Transfer learning** when you have limited labeled data but a similar pre-trained model exists.
- **Pipe mode** for large datasets (streams from S3, no disk space needed).
- **GPU instances (ml.p3)** for deep learning. CPU (ml.m5) for XGBoost.
- **Data parallelism** when data is large but model fits in one GPU.
- **Model parallelism** when model is too large for one GPU.
- **K-Fold cross validation** for small datasets (rotate validation set).
- **Never use accuracy** for imbalanced data. It's misleading.
- **Random Cut Forest** = SageMaker's anomaly detection algorithm.
- **Factorization Machines** = recommendations with sparse data (click-through prediction).
- **DeepAR** = time series forecasting (multiple related series, probabilistic).
- **Linear Learner** supports both classification AND regression (set predictor_type).
- **Early stopping** is the simplest regularization technique.

column2: +-- Classification: Binary (XGBoost, Linear Learner), Multi-class (XGBoost, KNN)
+-- Anomaly: Random Cut Forest, IP Insights
+-- Vision: Image Classification, Object Detection, Semantic Segmentation
+-- XGBoost: Tabular data king, handles missing values, classification + regression
+-- Linear Learner: Simple relationships, fast, supports L1/L2
+-- Bias-Variance: Underfitting (high bias) vs. Overfitting (high variance)
+-- Regularization: L1 (selection), L2 (shrinkage), Dropout, Early Stopping
+-- Data Split: Train/Val/Test (70/15/15), K-Fold for small data
+-- Infrastructure: File/Pipe/FastFile mode, GPU vs. CPU
+-- Distributed: Data parallelism, Model parallelism, Horovod
+-- SageMaker HPO: Objective metric, ranges, max jobs, warm start
+-- XGBoost Tuning: max_depth, eta, subsample, colsample_bytree, min_child_weight
+-- Best Practice: Start random, refine with Bayesian
+-- Classification: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1, AUC-ROC, AUC-PR
+-- Confusion Matrix: TP, TN, FP, FN
+-- Imbalanced: Never accuracy; use F1, AUC-PR
+-- Concepts: Epochs, Batch Size, Learning Rate, Activation Functions
+-- Regularization: Dropout, Batch Norm, Data Augmentation

column4: Match problem type to algorithm family
CNN, ResNet, transfer learning, GPU instances
